EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused runoff and flooded roadways. Two homes were flooded in the village of Tafuna due to poor drainages. The WSO recieved 6.41 inches of rainfall during this event.
Heavy rainfall caused runoff and flooded roadways. Two homes were flooded in the village of Tafuna due to poor drainages. The WSO recieved 6.41 inches of rainfall during this event.

The South Pacific Convergence Zone (SPCZ) generated heavy rainfall and strong gusty winds up to 44 mph. Reports of runoff, flooded roadways and landslides on the Island of Tutuila were results of an active SPCZ in the Samoan region. The Weather Service received 3.81 during this event.

The South Pacific Convergence Zone (SPCZ) generated heavy rainfall and strong gusty winds up to 44 mph. Reports of runoff, flooded roadways and landslides on the Island of Tutuila were results of an active SPCZ in the Samoan region. The Weather Service received 3.81 during this event.

On January 9th, a ridge of high pressure was formed northeast of the islands. The ridge strengthened and remained nearly stationary. Just to the southwest of the islands, a monsoonal trough was also intensifying and remaining nearly stationary. Numerous lows within the monsoonal trough were moving southeast. Therefore, the islands were on the gusty side of the monsoonal trough enforced by a ridge. WSO Pago Pago recorded sustained winds of 30 kt with the gusts of 43 kt. The episode occurred on the 9th to the 13th of the month. The Department of Public Works reported branches and debris on roads. There were no other damages reported.

Various low pressure systems that moved south of the islands had inundated low lying areas of the islands. In the upper levels, strong short waves aloft of Tonga were also moving eastward. On the 10th, 14th and 17th of the month were recorded with an average of 4 inches per day. WSO Pago had issued small stream flood advisory during these dates. The Department of Public Safety reported sewage overflow as well as various streams around the islands. The Department of Public Works reported major reconstructions of the islands main road due to large pot-holes and landslides around mountainous areas. No other damages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy showers had inundated low lying areas of the islands on the 10th, 14th, and the 17th of the month. Landslides and large pot-holes on the main road were the major reconstruction reported by the Department of Public Works. WSO Pago issued a Small Stream Flood Advisory prior to these event dates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Substantial vertical developments embedded ahead of the cold front had inundated low lying areas of the islands on the 27th of the month. The cold front was associated with a cut off low that situated east of New Zealand. The cold front was located just south south of the islands with a strong high pressure system over Fiji and a ridge of high pressure strengthening far east of the islands. RAOB at 00Z depicted a moist and an unstable atmosphere for the islands. WSO Pago Pago issued a flood advisory that was in effect for 6 hours. Furthermore, a 2.92 inches of precipitation was recorded at the station within 2 hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainshowers from a cold front had inundated low lying areas of the islands. The Department of Public Safety reported runoffs of several streams around the islands that had inundated low lying areas and caused difficulties of driving. WSO Pago Pago recorded 2.92 inches of precipitation within a 2 hour period. No other damages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused landslides in six villages on the Island of Tutuila. Runoff and flooded roads have created traffic havoc for Western villages on the Island of Tutuila. The Weather Service office received over 2 inches of rainfall during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A surface trough near Tutuila generated widespread showers over Tutuila Aunuu and Manu'a Islands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tsunami wave affected all of American Samoa including neighboring Samoan Islands.|The tsunami was generated by a 7.9 earthquake that occurred about 120 miles southwest of Pago Pago. The wave went inland as far as 1 mile. The height of the waves ranged from 15 feet at Pago Pago harbor to 40 feet at Poloa village on the Island of Tutuila. There were 32 confirmed deaths and 2 are still missing to date. Several marinas and vehicles were heavily damaged; businesses, homes, schools and coastal roads were either flooded or destroyed. The American Samoa Power plant was also damaged on the Island of Tutuila, and the government graveyard site was unearthed by strong tsunami waves.These damages amounted to $81 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 7.9 earthquake occurred about 120 miles southwest of Pago Pago and it shook the |entire Samoan and Tongan region. The earthquake occurred around 648 am |Samoan local time, and it took within 15 minutes for the tsunami waves to reach the Samoan Islands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall flooded several parks and roadways on the Island of Tutuila. Most villages were affected by increasing runoff due to poor drainages along the main road. The WSO received 4.65 inches of rainfall during this event.

Heavy rainfall flooded several parks and roadways on the Island of Tutuila. Most villages were affected by increasing runoff due to poor drainages along the main road. The WSO received 4.65 inches of rainfall during this event.

A strong pressure gradient over the islands generated by an active monsoonal trough southwest of the islands and a streghtening ridge located northeast of the islands. Northwesterly winds of 30 to 35 kt with gust up to 50 kt were generated by the strong pressure gradient. The episodes lasted for 4 days (1 April - 4 April). Several island farmers reported minor crop damages, for instance, bananas, cucumbers, taros, breadfruits and etc. The Department of Public Safety reported tree branches and other kinds of debris on the roads. No other damages were reported.

A combination of swells from an intense low pressure system east New Zealand and gusty trades had impacted south facing shores of the islands. Surf heights of 10 to 15 ft and higher sets were in effect from the 24th and later downgraded on the 27th of the month. WSO Pago Pago recorded trades of 30 mph with gusts up to 47 mph, which had facilitated the growth of surfs as they were near shores. The Department of Public Safety had reported numerous debris been washed over low lying areas of the eastern district of the island of Tutuila. Also, coastal erosions had a direct effect on various landslides that have been reported by village mayors. The Department of Public Works reported a major reconstruction or clean up of roads in the aftermath of the episode. The Department of Port and Administration had closed sea transportation among the islands of Tutuila, Aunuu and Manua on the 24th to 27th.  No other damages had been reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surf heights of 10 to 15 ft had impacted south facing shores of Tutuila. Low lying areas of the Eastern District had debris been washed over roads and coastal erosions which created some landslides along near shore mountain areas. The Shear Line that remained nearly stationary over the islands had facilitated with these landslides as ground became saturated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: WSO Pago recorded trades of 30 to 35 kt from the 24th to the 27th. The Department of Public Works reported a few uprooted trees around various areas. No other damages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active monsoonal trough of low pressure had slowly moved over the islands from the west. The trough produced heavy rainshowers in which had inundated low lying areas of the territory and thunderstorms on the 31st of March. The WSO Pago Pago recorded a precipitation daily high for the month of 3.09 inches. The Department of Public Safety reported inundations of low lying areas and severe runoffs of various streams from different areas around the islands. In addition, pot-holes and debris from overflow streams on roads were also reported. No other damages were reported.
